title: Research
text:
My research at the Paris Observatory revolves around discovering new exoplanets around nearby solar-type stars. Detecting planets around stars similar to the Sun will reveal the demographics of planets and show us how common or rare our solar system’s structure is.
Techniques used:

Astrometric data from the Gaia spacecraft
Direct imaging data from groud-based observatories like the Keck in Hawaii, and from space telescopes like the Hubble Space Telescope
Radial velocity (RV) data from surveys such as SOPHIE and HARPS

I have also performed transit photometry observations to analyse the transit timing variations (TTVs) as part of the ARIEL ExoClock Programme. This programme aimed to contribute to the ephemirides for the upcoming ARIEL space telescope. Our observations were performed from Observatoire des Baronnies Provençales and Deep Sky Chile.



As part of my MSc thesis, I have studied the population of stars in distant (high-redshift) galaxies using the James Webb Space Telescope. My work mainly involved using the spectroscopy and photometry collected by the NIRSpec and NIRCam instruments.

title: Blogs
text:
Blogs
I have written several science blogs aimed at a general audience, which can be found at: The Science Teen
These blogs aim to explain fascinating topics such as the existence of life on Earth in an easy-to-understand way without much technical jargon.

To learn more about Quarto websites visit https://quarto.org/docs/websites.

title: Outreach and Teaching
text:
I was elected the Observation Secretary for the Cambridge University Astronomical Society for two terms (2022-2024). I operated the telescopes in the observatory, conducted training sessions and certified members to use the 190-year-old Northumberland Telescope. I also conducted several public stargazing nights, including one at a primary school in a nearby town.



I gained experience as an assistant Physics teacher for Year 9 GCSE students at a local secondary school in Cambridge. I wrote a report titled “The effects of Inquiry-based learning on Physics Education” based on the outcome of my designed lesson.



I have presented experiments at the Cambridge Hands-On Science fairs to school children on topics including robotics, polarisation, air pressure, scattering, and infrared light. I have also volunteered for the Sussex Astronomy department’s stall at the Lewes STEM Fair and presented our astronomy research to the public.



I taught MIT’s block-based programming language ‘Scratch’ to Year 4 students at public schools in Oslo. I conducted the lessons in Norwegian and received pedagogical training from the Oslo Education Department (Utdanningsetaten).

title: About Me
text:
Research Engineer for CNRS in Exoplanetary Science at the Paris Observatory

 

I work on detecting new exoplanets using astrometry, direct imaging and radial velocities. I am part of the COBREX project, which is funded by the European Research Council (ERC) under the European Union’s Horizon 2020 research and innovation program.

title: About Me
text:
ARIEL ExoClock Training Programme
Observatoire des Baronnies Provençales
May 2026

Received training in performing observations at the Observatoire des Baronnies Provençales in the French Prealps.
Obtained exoplanet transit photometry by performing remote observations from Deep Sky Chile.
Analysed time-series using HOPS and Muniwin to detect transit timing variations (TTVs).

title: Aniruddha Girish Aramanekoppa
text: Hello! I am an astronomy research engineer at the Paris Observatory. I work on the detection of new exoplanets around nearby stars using several techniques. Check out my About Me and Research pages for more information about my work!
